subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for July 17, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - Persistent tissue hypoperfusion improves risk stratification b
sentences: Story 1 is about Persistent tissue hypoperfusion improves risk stratification beyond vasopressor dose in refractory septic shock: a secondary analysis of the ANDROMEDA-SHOCK-2 trial..
This is a recent research article from Intensive care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret stent tissue hypoperfusion with vasopressor dose after protocolized resuscitation improves mortality risk stratification compared with vasopressor dose alone. We performed an exploratory secondary analysis of the ANDROMEDA-SHOCK-2 trial. After 6 h of hemo?
Among 1363 patients with complete data, 188 (13.8%) had NEE > 0.5 µg/kg/min at 6-h, with 47.9% mortality.
Fifty-three patients (3.9%) were classified as refractory septic shock under the two-hypoperfusion criteria and 124 (9.1%) under the ≥ 1-hypoperfusion criterion.

Mortality was 73.6% (39/53) among patients meeting two-hypoperfusion criteria, compared with 23.7% (310/1310) among those classified as non-refractory according to these criteria. (aHR, 4.68; 95% CI 3.31-6.64;p < 0.001).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Effects of Abdominal Electrical Stimulation Combined With Airw
sentences: Story 2 is about Effects of Abdominal Electrical Stimulation Combined With Airway Clearance Techniques in Tracheotomized Patients With Neurological Disorders: A Randomized, Controlled Trial..
This is a randomized / clinical trial from Critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret min; 95% CI, 28.83-50.04; p < 0.001) and abdominal muscle thickness (difference, 0.106 cm; 95% CI, 0.08-0.13; p < 0.001) compared with the control. Furthermore, the decannulation success rate was significantly higher in the experimental group (93.3%) than?
We randomized 68 patients into control (n = 34) and experimental (n = 34) groups.

At 6 weeks, the experimental group demonstrated significantly higher peak involuntary cough flow rate (mean difference, 39.433 L/min; 95% CI, 28.83-50.04; p < 0.001) and abdominal muscle thickness (difference, 0.106 cm; 95% CI, 0.08-0.13; p < 0.001) compared with the control.
Furthermore, the decannulation success rate was significantly higher in the experimental group (93.3%) than that in the control group (73.3%; odds ratio, 5.070; 95% CI, 1.41-18.24; p = 0.013).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use this for journal club before practice change; decide whether the measured outcome is important enough for bedside decisions.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - Readmission and Late Mortality Among Children With Congenital
sentences: Story 3 is about Readmission and Late Mortality Among Children With Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia..
This is a recent research article from JAMA network open.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Participants were children with CDH who underwent surgical repair within the first 6 months of life, and were discharged alive from the primary stay, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?

Of the 1028 included infants (median [IQR] birth weight, 3050 [2720-3410] g; 849 [82.6%] with full-term birth; 630 [61.3%] male infants), 753 had at least 3 years of follow-up (median [IQR] time of follow-up, 6.2 [2.6-9.1] years), constituting the overall sample size for the primary analysis.
Of them, 546 children (72.5%) were readmitted at least once, and 182 (24.2%) required intensive care.

At 3 years, 208 (38.0%), 112 (20.5%), and 127 (23.3%) children had experienced at least 1 readmission for respiratory causes, gastrointestinal and/or nutritional issues, and CDH-related surgical complications, respectively.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: Effects of Abdominal Electrical Stimulation Combined With Airway Clearance Techniques in Tracheotomized Patients With Neurological Disorders: A Randomized, Controlled Trial.
articles.summary: Airway clearance techniques with or without abdominal electrical stimulation therapy. We randomized 68 patients into control (n = 34) and experimental (n = 34) groups. At 6 weeks, the experimental group demonstrated significantly higher peak involuntary cough flow rate (mean difference, 39.433 L/min; 95% CI, 28.83-50.04; p < 0.001) and abdominal muscle thickness (difference, 0.106 cm; 95% CI, 0.08-0.13; p < 0.001) compared with the control. Furthermore, the decannulation success rate was significantly higher in the experimental group (93.3%) than that in the control group (73.3%; odds ratio, 5.070; 95% CI, 1.41-18.24; p = 0.013). However, no significant differences were observed between the two groups in clinical pulmonary infection scores (p > 0.05) or resting diaphragm mobility (p > 0.05).

keyResults: We randomized 68 patients into control (n = 34) and experimental (n = 34) groups.

At 6 weeks, the experimental group demonstrated significantly higher peak involuntary cough flow rate (mean difference, 39.433 L/min; 95% CI, 28.83-50.04; p < 0.001) and abdominal muscle thickness (difference, 0.106 cm; 95% CI, 0.08-0.13; p < 0.001) compared with the control.
Furthermore, the decannulation success rate was significantly higher in the experimental group (93.3%) than that in the control group (73.3%; odds ratio, 5.070; 95% CI, 1.41-18.24; p = 0.013).
However, no significant differences were observed between the two groups in clinical pulmonary infection scores (p > 0.05) or resting diaphragm mobility (p > 0.05).
criticalAppraisal: Interventional evidence: check allocation, blinding, protocol adherence, and absolute effect size.
Check randomization, concealment, blinding, missing data, and whether analysis was intention-to-treat.

articles.title: Readmission and Late Mortality Among Children With Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia.
articles.summary: Of the 1028 included infants (median [IQR] birth weight, 3050 [2720-3410] g; 849 [82.6%] with full-term birth; 630 [61.3%] male infants), 753 had at least 3 years of follow-up (median [IQR] time of follow-up, 6.2 [2.6-9.1] years), constituting the overall sample size for the primary analysis. Of them, 546 children (72.5%) were readmitted at least once, and 182 (24.2%) required intensive care. At 3 years, 208 (38.0%), 112 (20.5%), and 127 (23.3%) children had experienced at least 1 readmission for respiratory causes, gastrointestinal and/or nutritional issues, and CDH-related surgical complications, respectively. Preterm birth (incidence rate ratio [IRR], 1.32; 95% CI, 1.10-1.60), associated congenital anomalies (IRR, 1.31; 95% CI, 1.13-1.53), a primary stay longer than 1 month (IRR, 1.50; 95% CI, 1.27-1.76), oxygen therapy at discharge (IRR, 2.14; 95% CI, 1.55-2.99), and enteral feeding at discharge (IRR, 2.21; 95% CI, 1.83-2.68) were independently associated with readmission. Fourteen late deaths (14 of 1028 infants [1.4%]) were recorded, attributable to CDH-related complications or associated comorbidities in half of cases.
articles.analysis: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.

pico.population: Participants were children with CDH who underwent surgical repair within the first 6 months of life, and were discharged alive from the primary stay
pico.intervention: Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective.
pico.comparator: Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.
pico.outcomes: The main outcomes were readmission to an acute care facility within 3 years after discharge and death during follow-up. Factors associated with readmission were identified using multivariable analysis.

keyResults: Of the 1028 included infants (median [IQR] birth weight, 3050 [2720-3410] g; 849 [82.6%] with full-term birth; 630 [61.3%] male infants), 753 had at least 3 years of follow-up (median [IQR] time of follow-up, 6.2 [2.6-9.1] years), constituting the overall sample size for the primary analysis.
Of them, 546 children (72.5%) were readmitted at least once, and 182 (24.2%) required intensive care.

At 3 years, 208 (38.0%), 112 (20.5%), and 127 (23.3%) children had experienced at least 1 readmission for respiratory causes, gastrointestinal and/or nutritional issues, and CDH-related surgical complications, respectively.

Preterm birth (incidence rate ratio [IRR], 1.32; 95% CI, 1.10-1.60), associated congenital anomalies (IRR, 1.31; 95% CI, 1.13-1.53), a primary stay longer than 1 month (IRR, 1.50; 95% CI, 1.27-1.76), oxygen therapy at discharge (IRR, 2.14; 95% CI, 1.55-2.99), and enteral feeding at discharge (IRR, 2.21; 95% CI, 1.83-2.68) were independently associated with readmission.
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.
